findings.summary: infrastructure/core/multi_project.py deferred-imports infrastructure.reporting inside method bodies, reversing the stated core→reporting dependency direction
evidence: multi_project.py lines 170-174: `from infrastructure.reporting import collect_output_statistics, generate_pipeline_report, save_pipeline_report` inside a method body
multi_project.py line 310: `from infrastructure.reporting import generate_multi_project_report` inside another method body
The deferred placement disguises a layer violation that would otherwise be a circular import
findings.suggestion: Move report generation calls into the PipelineExecutor or a dedicated orchestration layer that sits above both core and reporting; do not import reporting inside core modules

findings.summary: infrastructure/publishing/core.py re-exports calculate_file_hash from infrastructure.core.file_operations, leaking a core utility through a publishing boundary
evidence: publishing/core.py line 48: `from infrastructure.core.file_operations import calculate_file_hash  # noqa: F401`
publishing/__init__.py lines 16 and 61 re-export `calculate_file_hash` as if it is a publishing concept
The function lives in core/file_operations.py but callers can import it from infrastructure.publishing
findings.suggestion: Remove the re-export; callers that need calculate_file_hash should import it directly from infrastructure.core.file_operations

findings.summary: scripts/__init__.py dynamically exec_module-loads 06_llm_review.py and re-exports its internals, making the scripts package a de-facto library imported by infra tests
evidence: scripts/__init__.py lines 117-126: `importlib.util.spec_from_file_location` + `exec_module` to load 06_llm_review.py, then `main = _llm_review.main` and `ReviewMode = _llm_review.ReviewMode`
tests/infra_tests/llm/test_llm_review.py line 32: `from scripts import (...)` — infra tests depend on the scripts package
The thin-orchestrator contract states scripts should not be imported; here they are a library
findings.suggestion: Move ReviewMode and the LLM review public API fully into infrastructure.llm; have the script import from there; remove the dynamic loader from scripts/__init__.py

findings.summary: PerformanceMetrics and PerformanceMonitor are defined twice in core/performance.py and core/performance_monitor.py with incompatible schemas, requiring aliased re-exports
evidence: core/performance.py line 44: `class PerformanceMetrics` with field `duration: float`
core/performance_monitor.py line 22: `class PerformanceMetrics` with field `operation_name: str, execution_time: float`
core/__init__.py lines 91-104: canonical names resolve to performance.py; AdvancedPerformanceMetrics/AdvancedPerformanceMonitor alias performance_monitor.py
Two classes with the same public name in the same package boundary
findings.suggestion: Merge into a single PerformanceMetrics in one module; deprecate and remove the other; eliminate the Advanced* aliases

findings.summary: infrastructure/publishing/core.py owns only one real function; all others are pass-through re-exports from sibling modules, duplicating publishing/__init__.py's role
evidence: publishing/core.py lines 24-48: six import-and-reexport blocks (noqa: F401) for citations, metadata, models, platforms, and core.file_operations
Only create_publication_package, create_submission_checklist, create_publication_announcement, validate_publication_readiness, generate_doi_badge are implemented in core.py
publishing/__init__.py then re-exports everything from core.py, adding a second re-export layer
findings.suggestion: Implement the four real functions directly in the submodules where their dependencies live; remove core.py as a re-export hub and have __init__.py import directly from submodules

findings.summary: scripts/__init__.py simultaneously acts as a menu registry, LLM function re-export hub, dynamic script loader, and pipeline orchestrator re-exporter with no single coherent role
evidence: Lines 33-89: MENU_SCRIPT_MAPPING registry
Lines 92-113: re-exports of infrastructure.llm functions
Lines 117-126: dynamic exec_module loader for 06_llm_review.py
Lines 148-153: re-exports of scripts.execute_pipeline functions
__all__ spans 26 names across 4 unrelated concerns
findings.suggestion: Remove all re-exports from scripts/__init__.py; leave only MENU_SCRIPT_MAPPING if needed for run.sh; move the LLM public API to infrastructure.llm.__init__

findings.summary: from __future__ import annotations is present in 95/126 infrastructure files but absent in 31, including all __init__.py files and scattered implementation modules
evidence: holistic_context sibling_behavior outliers list 18 __init__.py files and 13 implementation files missing annotations import
Sibling modules in the same directory mix presence/absence: infrastructure/core/security.py missing annotations while infrastructure/core/logging_utils.py has it
The pattern is dominant (95/126 = 75%) but unenforced, creating inconsistency
findings.suggestion: Add `from __future__ import annotations` to all infrastructure Python files; enforce via a pre-commit ruff or flake8 rule (FA100)

findings.summary: 3 of 18 scripts omit get_logger/log_header/log_success that 15 sibling scripts consistently use, creating logging protocol islands
evidence: holistic_context sibling_behavior scripts/ outliers: scripts/02_run_analysis.py missing get_logger, log_header, log_success; scripts/generate_test_summary.py missing the same three; scripts/__init__.py missing the same three
15/18 scripts share the pattern; these three are the only outliers
findings.suggestion: Add get_logger and log_header/log_success calls to the three outlier scripts to match the established sibling protocol

findings.summary: infrastructure/rendering/pdf_renderer.py imports re at module scope on line 5 then re-imports it inside 8 function bodies, mixing import placement conventions in one file
evidence: Line 5: `import re` at module level
Lines 37, 429, 589, 613, 948, 1317, 1422, 1771: `import re` repeated inside function bodies
No other infrastructure module follows this pattern
findings.suggestion: Remove the redundant in-function `import re` statements; the module-level import is sufficient

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/config_loader.py uses bare logging.getLogger in multiple places instead of the project-wide get_logger from infrastructure.core.logging_utils
evidence: config_loader.py line 99: `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` inside validate_config_keys
config_loader.py lines 145, 388, 434, 441, 449: additional bare logging.getLogger calls
All other infrastructure/core modules use `from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import get_logger` and call it once at module scope
findings.suggestion: Replace all bare logging.getLogger calls with a single module-level `logger = get_logger(__name__)` from infrastructure.core.logging_utils

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/dashboard_generator.py has 22 except Exception blocks that log-and-return None with no propagation, making chart failures invisible to callers
evidence: Lines 3404-3465: a second batch of 9 warning-level swallows around chart generation calls
Callers receive no signal that chart generation failed; the generated report silently omits charts
findings.suggestion: For chart functions called from the main report generator, either propagate the exception or return a typed Optional result that the caller checks; do not silently swallow at both the inner and outer call sites

findings.summary: infrastructure/documentation/ returns None on failure with no raises and no logging, while peer subsystems (rendering, scientific) raise custom exceptions
evidence: holistic_context errors.strategy_by_directory documentation/: try_catch=5, returns_null=2, throws=1 — almost no explicit error raising
Peer subsystems: rendering/ has throws=43, scientific/ has throws=23
figure_manager.py has 2 except Exception blocks that return None silently
findings.suggestion: Align documentation module error handling with peer subsystems: raise domain exceptions (e.g., ConfigurationError, FileOperationError) on recoverable boundaries rather than returning None

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ mixes 22 return-None paths with 122 raises, making call-site error handling unpredictable for the most-imported layer
evidence: holistic_context errors.strategy_by_directory core/: try_catch=162, throws=122, returns_null=22
config_loader.py load_config() returns None on both missing file (expected) and unexpected exceptions (line 143: bare except returns None silently)
environment.py check_dependencies() returns Tuple[bool, List[str]] while other environment functions raise on failure
file_operations.py clean_output_directory() returns bool False on error while calculate_file_hash() returns Optional[str]
findings.suggestion: Define a consistent policy per error category: use return None/bool only for expected absence (file not found); raise typed TemplateError subclasses for unexpected failures; document the policy in core/__init__.py

findings.summary: ProjectLogger is a pure passthrough wrapper over stdlib Logger with no added policy, making it an abstraction that adds indirection without value
evidence: Every method (debug, info, warning, error, critical) is a one-liner that calls self._logger.<method>(message, *args, **kwargs) verbatim
get_project_logger() returns ProjectLogger(name, level), which itself just calls get_logger(name) — two factory functions wrapping the same construction
setup_project_logging() calls get_project_logger() which calls get_logger() — three hops to reach a stdlib Logger
No retry, rate-limiting, context injection, or structured enrichment is added at any layer of the chain
findings.suggestion: Remove ProjectLogger and get_project_logger/setup_project_logging. Callers that need the extra methods (success, header, stage) can import those free functions directly from logging_utils; the stdlib Logger can be extended with a custom subclass if truly needed.

findings.summary: RenderManager exposes render_pdf, render_web, render_slides, render_combined_pdf, render_combined_web as single-line delegators to sub-renderer methods, adding a class boundary with no coordination logic
evidence: render_pdf() is a one-liner: return self.pdf_renderer.render(source_file)
render_web() is a one-liner: return self.web_renderer.render(source_file)
render_combined_pdf() logs one line then delegates: return self.pdf_renderer.render_combined(...)
render_combined_web() logs one line then delegates: return self.web_renderer.render_combined(...)
render_slides() is a one-liner forwarding to slides_renderer.render(...)
findings.suggestion: Either consolidate orchestration logic (format selection, error normalisation, output collection) into RenderManager so it earns its place, or collapse the sub-renderers into a single module and expose functions directly — the current two-level split amplifies call hops without reducing complexity.

findings.summary: get_config_as_dict and related helpers use opaque Dict[str,str] return types as implicit parameter bags rather than a typed config object, losing all schema benefits
evidence: get_config_as_dict() returns Dict[str, str] keyed by magic strings (PROJECT_TITLE, AUTHOR_NAME, AUTHOR_ORCID, etc.) with no schema enforcement
ResolvedTestingConfig frozen dataclass exists but get_testing_config() returns TestingConfig (TypedDict) and the function never uses ResolvedTestingConfig — the typed path is defined but bypassed
get_config_as_env_vars() filters the same dict by os.environ — callers cannot know which keys will be present without reading source
findings.suggestion: Return ResolvedTestingConfig from get_testing_config(), and introduce an analogous typed dataclass for the manuscript/author block so callers receive a validated, attribute-accessible object instead of a string dict.

findings.summary: The `re` module is imported at module level in pdf_renderer.py but then re-imported inside functions at least 7 times, creating redundant import statements throughout the file
evidence: Line 5: `import re` at module level
Line 37: `import re` inside _parse_missing_package_error()
Line 429: `import re` inside render_combined()'s validation block
Lines 589, 613: two more `import re` inside the CalledProcessError handler
Lines 948, 1317, 1422, 1771: additional inline imports found by grep
findings.suggestion: Remove all inline `import re` statements; the top-level import is sufficient. Apply the same audit to `import shutil` (also re-imported inline at line 300) and `import logging` inside pipeline.py's _flush_log_handlers.

findings.summary: find_config_file() resolves to `repo_root / 'project' / 'manuscript' / config.yaml` (singular 'project') but the actual layout uses `projects/{name}/manuscript/` — the function will always return None for real projects
evidence: Line 280: config_path = repo_root / 'project' / 'manuscript' / 'config.yaml'
All other config-reading functions (get_translation_languages, get_review_types) correctly use `repos_root / 'projects' / project_name / 'manuscript' / 'config.yaml'`
get_config_as_dict() calls find_config_file(), so the PROJECT_TITLE/AUTHOR_NAME env var population silently fails for every live project
findings.suggestion: Fix find_config_file() to accept a project_name parameter and resolve `repo_root / 'projects' / project_name / 'manuscript' / 'config.yaml'`, consistent with every other config-reading function in the module.

findings.summary: Pipeline stages are passed as raw untyped tuples (name, func, skip?) across execute_full_pipeline, execute_core_pipeline, and _resume_pipeline — the same construction is triplicated with positional indexing at every consumption site
evidence: execute_full_pipeline() builds stages as list[tuple] with 2- or 3-element tuples; _execute_pipeline() accesses [0], [1], and guards len(stage_spec) > 2 for the optional skip flag
_resume_pipeline() rebuilds an identical stage list from scratch (lines 347-365) duplicating the same construction already in execute_full_pipeline/execute_core_pipeline
The same len(stage_spec) > 2 guard appears three separate times: in _execute_pipeline(), in _resume_pipeline() stage scanning, and again in the remaining-stage loop
PipelineStageResult dataclass exists but there is no StageSpec dataclass — the boundary between stage declaration and execution is stringly-typed
findings.suggestion: Introduce a small StageSpec dataclass (name: str, func: Callable, skip: bool = False). Build the canonical stage list once in a private method, shared by execute_full_pipeline, execute_core_pipeline, and _resume_pipeline. This eliminates triplication and all positional-index guards.

findings.summary: _resume_pipeline() mutates PipelineConfig.resume directly to prevent recursion instead of using a local flag, leaking control flow across the boundary between the method and its callers
evidence: Lines 320-329: self.config.resume = False followed by try/finally to restore original_resume — PipelineConfig is a mutable dataclass being used as a recursion guard
The same pattern is repeated at lines 334-343 and 403-412 — three sites that temporarily disable a field on a shared config object
PipelineConfig has no frozen=True, so this mutation is invisible to callers
findings.suggestion: Add a private _executing: bool flag on PipelineExecutor (not on the config) to guard against recursive fallback, or refactor fallback paths as direct calls to an internal _run_fresh() that does not consult self.config.resume.

findings.summary: The CalledProcessError handler in render_combined() spans ~130 lines of inline error parsing, context building, source-file attribution, and character-level logging that belongs in extracted helpers
evidence: Lines 561-789: a single except block that (1) parses stderr/stdout for error lines, (2) extracts position integers with re.search, (3) reads and slices md_content for context, (4) walks source_files to attribute errors to a specific file, (5) emits a dozen logger.error calls, and (6) raises RenderingError
The brace-depth walk in the pre-validation block (lines 476-508) is an inline state machine with nested while loops and index arithmetic that could be a named function
import re appears inside both the except block (line 589) and within a nested if (line 613) despite being available at module scope
findings.suggestion: Extract: (1) _parse_pandoc_error(e) -> (error_lines, position_info), (2) _attribute_error_to_source(position_info, source_files, md_content) -> Optional[dict], (3) _count_unbalanced_braces(content) -> int. The except block becomes 10-15 lines that call these helpers.

findings.summary: Two try/except UnboundLocalError guards in _process_bibliography() wrap existence checks on variables that cannot actually be unbound at those points, adding dead defensive code
evidence: Lines 267-278: try: if not bib_file.exists() ... except UnboundLocalError: if not bib_file.exists() — bib_file is a parameter, it is always bound
Lines 285-292: same pattern for aux_file, which is assigned on line 282 unconditionally before the guard
Both except branches are verbatim copies of the try bodies — the guards can never fire but obscure the real logic
findings.suggestion: Remove both try/except UnboundLocalError blocks. The plain if not bib_file.exists() and if not aux_file.exists() checks are sufficient and correct.

findings.summary: ProjectLogger.debug() and .info() are one-liner pass-throughs with 4-line Args/Returns/None docstrings
evidence: Lines 145-156: debug() body is self._logger.debug(message, *args, **kwargs) — one line — with Args/Returns/None docstring block
Lines 158-169: info() is identical pattern with full Args/Returns/None boilerplate
Lines 196-205: header() one-liner with Args/Returns/None
warning(), critical(), progress(), stage(), substep() all one-liners but have inconsistently minimal docstrings — the verbose ones are clearly generated selectively
findings.suggestion: Strip docstrings from pure pass-through delegator methods to a single line or remove them entirely; the method name is self-documenting at this level

findings.summary: log_function_call inner wrapper() and decorator() functions carry full Args/Returns/Raises docstrings despite being unexported implementation details
evidence: Lines 589-616: decorator() inner function has a 4-line docstring with Args/Returns
Lines 600-616: wrapper() inner function has a 7-line docstring with Args/Returns/Raises
Both are unreachable from outside the closure and will never appear in generated docs
findings.suggestion: Remove docstrings from inner closure functions; they add no value and are a clear LLM generation artifact

findings.summary: A standalone docstring literal appears mid-file in logging_utils.py, after the class definition — not attached to any module, class, or function
evidence: Lines 58-116: a multi-paragraph string literal between class and module-level code, not assigned and not a valid docstring — it is dead code that documents intent but executes as a no-op string expression
findings.suggestion: Move this usage guide to the module-level docstring at line 1 or to a separate docs file; remove the orphaned string literal

findings.summary: format_error_with_suggestions has three branches in the context loop that all produce identical output
evidence: Lines 54-60: if key == 'file' ... elif key == 'line' ... else — all three branches append the same f-string f'   • {key}: {value}' with no differentiation
findings.suggestion: Collapse the three branches to a single append; the conditional is decorative boilerplate that signals copy-paste generation

findings.summary: Two incompatible PerformanceMetrics dataclasses exist in sibling modules with different field schemas, both exported from infrastructure/core/__init__.py
evidence: performance.py PerformanceMetrics: fields are duration, resource_usage, operations_count, cache_hits
performance_monitor.py PerformanceMetrics: fields are operation_name, execution_time, memory_peak, memory_current, memory_delta
__init__.py lines 101-103: exports the monitor version as AdvancedPerformanceMetrics — the alias normalizes the collision but exposes two incompatible schemas to callers
findings.suggestion: Merge into a single PerformanceMetrics class or clearly separate them into distinct subpackages with distinct names; the aliasing approach hides a real design fault

findings.summary: Codebase exposes two parallel logger APIs (get_logger -> logging.Logger, get_project_logger -> ProjectLogger) with no clear guidance on which to use
evidence: get_logger() returns stdlib logging.Logger — used throughout infrastructure/
get_project_logger() returns ProjectLogger wrapper — documented as 'recommended for projects'
ProjectLogger adds no policy over the stdlib logger; it only re-dispatches to the same underlying logger
__init__.py exports get_logger but not get_project_logger, yet logging_utils.__all__ includes both
findings.suggestion: Either make ProjectLogger add meaningful policy (structured context, automatic stage tracking) or eliminate it and have projects use get_logger() directly

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ logging subsystem is fragmented across 4 sibling files with logging_utils.py acting as a re-export umbrella for the others
evidence: logging_utils.py lines 24-35: imports Spinner, StreamingProgress, calculate_eta, calculate_eta_ema, calculate_eta_with_confidence, log_progress_bar, log_progress_streaming, log_with_spinner, JSONFormatter, TemplateFormatter, format_duration, format_error_with_suggestions all from siblings
infrastructure/core/__init__.py then re-exports from logging_utils — two hops of re-export for a flat package
findings.suggestion: Either keep one logging_utils.py with all logging code (remove the split) or move the logging subsystem into infrastructure/logging/ subpackage with a clean __init__.py; the current split adds navigation cost with no boundary benefit

findings.summary: test_monitor_with_memory_tracking contains an assertion that is always True, providing no coverage signal
evidence: Line 125: assert metrics.memory_peak is not None or metrics.memory_current is not None or True — the trailing `or True` makes the entire assertion unconditionally pass regardless of actual memory tracking outcome
findings.suggestion: Remove the `or True` guard and assert the actual expected post-condition, or skip the assertion and add a comment explaining why memory values are unpredictable in CI

findings.summary: Integration tests in test_optimizer.py use pytest.skip() inside the test body based on return values, masking actual failures
evidence: Lines 464-467, 483-486, 514-516, 530-535: pytest.skip('... returned None') is called when run_stability_analysis() or run_performance_benchmarking() return None
A None return from a function under test is a failure, not a skip condition — this hides real breakage from CI reporting
findings.suggestion: Assert the return value is not None before using it; if the infrastructure dependency is genuinely optional, use a session-scoped fixture that checks availability once and skips the whole class at collection time
